description: This neuron detects capitalized tokens that begin proper nouns, abbreviations, or the start of new topics/entries (often names of people, places, organizations, or technical terms).
This neuron detects capitalized single letters or short abbreviations that begin proper nouns, names, or technical terms at the start of titles, sentences, or document sections.
This neuron detects single capital letters that begin proper nouns, names, or technical terms, particularly at the start of titles, headings, or sentences.
This neuron detects the first letter of proper nouns, names, or specialized terms at the beginning of titles, sentences, or document sections.
This neuron detects the first token of a proper noun, title, or technical term, particularly at the beginning of titles, names, locations, or scientific/medical terminology.
This neuron detects the first letter or initial character of proper nouns, names, titles, and the beginning of new topics or entries.
This neuron detects the first capitalized letter or token at the beginning of proper nouns, brand names, technical terms, or the start of new sentences/sections.
This neuron detects past participle verbs or adjectives that describe states, conditions, or qualities being attributed to something.
This neuron detects hyphens or dashes that appear within citation references or hyperlink syntax, particularly in academic or technical text formatting.
This neuron detects line breaks or newlines that separate distinct sections, titles, or segments of text, often marking transitions between different topics or formatting contexts.
This neuron detects periods that end academic, technical, or formal article titles and abstracts, typically appearing at the boundary between a title/header and the beginning of the main text.
This neuron detects periods or dots that serve as delimiters in version numbers, file extensions, or as sentence-ending punctuation in formal or technical text.

This neuron detects line breaks or newlines that occur after a partial word or sentence fragment, particularly in HTML markup, forum posts, article titles, or structured text where content continues on the next line.
This neuron detects periods that mark the end of a complete sentence or independent clause, particularly when followed by content that continues or elaborates on the same topic.
This neuron detects sentence-ending periods that are followed by text providing additional context, elaboration, or examples related to the preceding statement.
This neuron detects sentence-ending periods, particularly those that mark the conclusion of a complete thought or statement before transitioning to a new topic or sentence.
This neuron detects periods (dots) used as separators in version numbers, package names, and domain-like identifiers in programming contexts.
This neuron detects words that continue or complete a compound term, technical phrase, or multi-word expression, particularly in medical, scientific, or technical contexts.

This neuron detects sentence-ending periods that conclude a complete thought or statement, particularly in formal or informational text where the period marks a natural transition point before a new topic or elaboration begins.
This neuron detects line breaks or paragraph boundaries, particularly those that separate distinct sections of text or precede new content blocks.
This neuron detects line breaks or newlines that occur after version numbers, package paths, case citations, or other technical identifiers in code and formal documents.
This neuron detects sentence-ending periods that conclude the title, heading, or introductory statement of an academic, technical, or formal document before transitioning to the main body text.

This neuron detects periods that mark the end of a complete sentence or thought, particularly in formal or academic writing contexts where one sentence concludes before transitioning to a new but related topic.

This neuron detects tokens that are part of file paths, directory names, method names, or identifiers in technical/programming contexts, often appearing after forward slashes or in camelCase notation.
This neuron detects periods that mark the end of complete sentences or independent clauses, particularly when followed by a new sentence that continues or elaborates on the previous topic.
This neuron detects words that represent categories, fields, or domains of activity, often appearing as labels or section headers in titles and text.

This neuron detects newline characters or line breaks that appear after titles, headers, dates, or other introductory text elements, typically marking the transition from metadata or heading to the main content.
This neuron detects capitalized words or proper nouns that appear to be titles, names, or section headers, particularly when they start a phrase or are used as labels.
This neuron detects opening square brackets that typically introduce titles, citations, or bracketed references at the beginning of academic articles, medical literature, or song credits.
This neuron detects sentence-ending periods, particularly those that conclude complex or formal statements in academic, technical, or journalistic writing.
